main_summary.summary: Reverend Joseph Echols Lowery, SCLC co-founder and longtime civil rights leader, was interviewed by Joe Mosnier on June 6, 2011, at Cascade United Methodist Church in Atlanta, Georgia, for the Civil Rights History Project. Lowery describes his pastorate at Warren Street Methodist Church in Mobile, Alabama, beginning in 1952, his early work with the NAACP under John LeFlore, and the Mobile bus desegregation ride on the Prichard route alongside Reverend S.M. McCree. He recounts meeting with C.K. Steele in Tallahassee, Kelly Miller Smith in Nashville, T.J. Jemison, and Fred Shuttlesworth to organize a South-wide movement, the interrupted Atlanta meeting after Ralph Abernathy's church was bombed, and SCLC's founding in New Orleans. He details the New York Times v. Sullivan libel suit, the seizure and auction of his car, the role of attorney M. Roland Nachman, and the Supreme Court reversal. Lowery describes the Selma campaign, Jimmie Lee Jackson's death in Marion, Bloody Sunday, the march to Montgomery, leading the committee that confronted Governor George Wallace, the Voting Rights Act of 1965, and delivering the benediction at Barack Obama's inauguration.
key_themes: Mobile bus desegregation and Prichard route
New York Times v. Sullivan libel suit
Selma to Montgomery march and Voting Rights Act
main_summary.historical_significance:
Lowery is a primary source on the founding moments of SCLC, including the interrupted Atlanta organizing meeting and the relocation of the founding convention to New Orleans after the bombing of Ralph Abernathy's church. As one of the four named defendants in the Sullivan libel case alongside Abernathy, Shuttlesworth, and S.S. Seay, he provides direct testimony on the property-seizure phase that preceded the Supreme Court reversal, including the auction of his confiscated car and its purchase by R.B. DeWitt of the Mobile YMCA.

The interview also documents Lowery's role at the climax of the Selma campaign as chairman of the committee appointed by Dr. King to present the march's demands to Governor George Wallace, and his account of the confrontation at the Alabama State Capitol with state troopers and the National Guard. His recollections of being an early civil-rights-leadership supporter of Barack Obama, traveling to Selma in 2007 for the Brown Chapel AME event where Obama claimed the Joshua-generation mantle, and giving the benediction at the 2009 inauguration anchor the throughline from Montgomery to the first Black presidency.

chapters.title: Mobile Pastorate and the NAACP
chapters.summary: Lowery describes arriving at Warren Street Methodist Church in Mobile, Alabama, in 1952, joining the NAACP meetings already held in his sanctuary, and working under local leader John LeFlore. He contrasts Mobile's less toxic racism with that of Birmingham and Montgomery and credits Roman Catholic city commissioner Joe Langan with enabling quieter changes.
related_events: Mobile city commission under Joe Langan

chapters.title: The Prichard Bus Ride
chapters.summary: As president of the Interdenominational Ministerial Alliance, Lowery rode the Prichard bus route with Reverend S.M. McCree to test desegregation after Mobile clergy held workshops led by Fellowship of Reconciliation trainers. He recounts confronting a hostile passenger by speaking directly to him about mutual safety, ending the ride in Prichard, and meeting Black women who had ridden in the back so long they were uncertain how to respond.

chapters.title: Founding SCLC
chapters.summary: Lowery describes how the Montgomery Improvement Association formed in response to the bus boycott, how Governor John Patterson outlawed the NAACP in Alabama by demanding the membership roll, and how leaders including Fred Shuttlesworth, C.K. Steele in Tallahassee, Kelly Miller Smith in Nashville, and T.J. Jemison met in Atlanta to organize a South-wide movement. The Atlanta meeting was interrupted by the bombing of Ralph Abernathy's church, prompting reconvening in New Orleans, where SCLC was officially founded with Dr. King as president and Lowery elected secretary and later vice president. Bayard Rustin came to Montgomery several times to draft the founding documents.
related_events: Governor Patterson outlaws NAACP in Alabama

chapters.title: Dr. King and Ella Baker
chapters.summary: Lowery reflects on Dr. King's emergence in Montgomery as the first leader of a successful boycott in a city of 50,000 Black residents and on King's commitment to nonviolence even after his house was bombed. He credits Ella Baker as SCLC's first full-time executive director after Rev. John L. Tilley's brief tenure and notes that her conflicts with the preachers stemmed in part from male discomfort with strong women. Baker's leadership inspired the students who organized SNCC.

chapters.title: Theology of Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Lowery explains nonviolence in both practical and theological terms: unarmed and outnumbered, Black activists could not have won a violent confrontation, while the more white organizations responded violently the more they were criticized worldwide. Theologically, he cites Jesus on the cross asking forgiveness for those who nailed him, the New Testament command to love one's enemies, and George Wallace's own astonishment at the nonviolent spirit of those who met with him. Lowery describes nonviolence as transformative inwardly as well as outwardly.

chapters.title: The Sullivan Libel Suit
chapters.summary: Lowery describes the 1959 libel suit that led to the seizure of his car after officials in Montgomery could not attach his church-parsonage residence. He recalls being asked on the stand where he was born and answering Madison rather than Huntsville to protect inherited property his sister and he held in Madison County. He details attorney M. Roland Nachman's summation, which named Sammy Davis Jr. and his marriage to May Britt to inflame the jury, and the New York Times' refusal to extend its supersedeas bond to the four ministerial co-defendants (Abernathy, Shuttlesworth, Seay, and himself). The Supreme Court later reversed the verdict. Mobile's Methodist congregants bought back his auctioned car for $8,900 through YMCA secretary R.B. DeWitt.

chapters.title: Sit-Ins and Public Accommodations
chapters.summary: Lowery describes the shift from voter registration and bus desegregation to broader attacks on segregation through sit-ins beginning with Greensboro in 1960. He recounts demonstrating at Morrison's Cafeteria in Nashville, where the chain president told Tennessee's governor he would never desegregate over his dead body but the chain desegregated in July 1964 a month before his death. Lowery frames the Montgomery boycott as the beginning of an era of self-determination in which Black Americans took responsibility 'in their own hands.'

chapters.title: Selma and the Wallace Confrontation
chapters.summary: Lowery describes the Selma campaign decision made at the A.G. Gaston Motel in Birmingham after President Lyndon B. Johnson asked them to wait on further civil-rights legislation. He identifies Dallas County's majority-Black population without registered voters as the strategic choice, recounts Jimmie Lee Jackson's death in Marion as the catalyst for the march to Montgomery, and Bloody Sunday on the bridge. As chairman of the committee Dr. King appointed to deliver the march's demands to Governor George Wallace (a committee chosen because Lowery was pastoring in Birmingham, satisfying Wallace's Alabama-only condition), he describes the standoff with state troopers, the National Guard parting 'the blue sea,' a 90-minute meeting in which Wallace tore paper into scraps, and the passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
related_events: Jimmie Lee Jackson's death in Marion

chapters.title: Obama and the Inauguration Benediction
chapters.summary: Lowery describes becoming an early civil-rights-leadership supporter of Barack Obama when most of his peers including Andrew Young and John Lewis backed Hillary Clinton. He recalls attending the 2007 Brown Chapel AME event in Selma where Obama named the older leaders the 'Moses generation' and himself the 'Joshua generation,' his subsequent travels to campaign in North Carolina, Virginia, Alabama, Georgia, and Iowa, and Obama's phone call asking him to give the benediction at the inauguration. Lowery describes giving the benediction on a cold January day, hearing in the Star-Spangled Banner a sound that had not changed though the country had, and connecting the moment to Dr. King's summons forty years earlier.
